comment_text.sn11.3:1.1: This discourse invokes several major Vedic deities invoked as martial deities, gods of war. Compare their mention at DN 13:25.2, where these deities, though aristocrat warriors, are invoked by brahmins. In Theravada it is a popular protection chant (paritta) included in the Maha Pirit Potha.
comment_text.sn11.3:3.1: Compare the martial exhortation at Rig Veda 10.103.11: “Ours is Indra when the banners clash.” Varāhamihira tells the full story of Indra’s banner at Bṛhatsaṁhitā 42.
comment_text.sn11.3:4.1: Pajāpati (“progenitor”) was the lonely and “undefined” (anirukta) god of creation (“Let Prajāpati generate progeny for us”, Rig Veda 10.85.3). The heat of his fervent exertions (tapas) created the world and all things in it (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 6).
comment_text.sn11.3:5.1: Varuṇa is invoked frequently in the Vedas, and in the suttas he is associated with other leading deities such as Indra and Soma (DN 13:25.2, DN 20:13.3,DN 32:10.2). As one of the twelve children of Aditi he stood for a calendar month, and grew a wide and sometimes baffling array of associations—the oceans, water, the sky (at night), and justice. Vedic Varuṇa was the god of command, the king of tough rule. He was identified with the aristocrats, his partner Mitra with the brahmins (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 4.1.4).
comment_text.sn11.3:6.1: Īsāna (Sanskrit Īśāna) was created by Pajāpati as “president”, said to be the sun (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 6.1.3). He was also identified with Rudra and Śiva.
comment_text.sn11.3:14.2: Narāsabha is literally “a bull of a man”, but the predominant sense is “chief, lord, captain” rather than “bull”. Vṛṣabha was widely used in this sense as far back as the Rig Veda. The same is true of its synonyms janavasabha and purisanisabha, but not purisūsabha at Bu Pj 1:10.21.4.

root_text.sn11.3:2.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo deve tāvatiṁse āmantesi:
root_text.sn11.3:3.1: ‘Sace, mārisā, devānaṁ saṅgāmagatānaṁ uppajjeyya bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, mameva tasmiṁ samaye dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha.
root_text.sn11.3:3.2: Mamañhi vo dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissati.
root_text.sn11.3:4.1: No ce me dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha, atha pajāpatissa devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha.
root_text.sn11.3:4.2: Pajāpatissa hi vo devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissati.
root_text.sn11.3:5.1: No ce pajāpatissa devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha, atha varuṇassa devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha.
root_text.sn11.3:5.2: Varuṇassa hi vo devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissati.
root_text.sn11.3:6.1: No ce varuṇassa devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha, atha īsānassa devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokeyyātha.
root_text.sn11.3:6.2: Īsānassa hi vo devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissatī’ti.
root_text.sn11.3:7.1: Taṁ kho pana, bhikkhave, sakkassa vā devānamindassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ, pajāpatissa vā devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ, varuṇassa vā devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ, īsānassa vā devarājassa dhajaggaṁ ullokayataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyethāpi nopi pahīyetha.
root_text.sn11.3:8.2: Sakko hi, bhikkhave, devānamindo avītarāgo avītadoso avītamoho bhīru chambhī utrāsī palāyīti.
root_text.sn11.3:9.2: ‘sace tumhākaṁ, bhikkhave, araññagatānaṁ vā rukkhamūlagatānaṁ vā suññāgāragatānaṁ vā uppajjeyya bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, mameva tasmiṁ samaye anussareyyātha:
root_text.sn11.3:9.3: “itipi so bhagavā arahaṁ sammāsambuddho vijjācaraṇasampanno sugato lokavidū anuttaro purisadammasārathi satthā devamanussānaṁ buddho bhagavā”ti.
root_text.sn11.3:9.4: Mamañhi vo, bhikkhave, anussarataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissati.
root_text.sn11.3:10.1: No ce maṁ anussareyyātha, atha dhammaṁ anussareyyātha:
root_text.sn11.3:10.2: “svākkhāto bhagavatā dhammo sandiṭṭhiko akāliko ehipassiko opaneyyiko paccattaṁ veditabbo viññūhī”ti.
root_text.sn11.3:10.3: Dhammañhi vo, bhikkhave, anussarataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissati.
root_text.sn11.3:11.1: No ce dhammaṁ anussareyyātha, atha saṅghaṁ anussareyyātha:
root_text.sn11.3:11.2: “suppaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho ujuppaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho ñāyappaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho sāmīcippaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, yadidaṁ cattāri purisayugāni aṭṭha purisapuggalā esa bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, āhuneyyo pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassā”ti.
root_text.sn11.3:11.3: Saṅghañhi vo, bhikkhave, anussarataṁ yaṁ bhavissati bhayaṁ vā chambhitattaṁ vā lomahaṁso vā, so pahīyissati.
root_text.sn11.3:12.2: Tathāgato hi, bhikkhave, arahaṁ sammāsambuddho vītarāgo vītadoso vītamoho abhīru acchambhī anutrāsī apalāyī’”ti.
root_text.sn11.3:12.4: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:

translation_text.sn11.3:1.2: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn11.3:2.1: “Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans.
translation_text.sn11.3:2.2: Then Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the gods of the thirty-three:
translation_text.sn11.3:3.1: ‘Good fellows, when the gods are fighting, if you get scared or terrified, just look up at my banner’s crest.
translation_text.sn11.3:3.2: Then your fear and terror will go away.
translation_text.sn11.3:4.1: If you can’t see my banner’s crest, then look up at the banner’s crest of the Progenitor, king of gods.
translation_text.sn11.3:4.2: Then your fear and terror will go away.
translation_text.sn11.3:5.1: If you can’t see his banner’s crest, then look up at the banner’s crest of Varuṇa, king of gods.
translation_text.sn11.3:5.2: Then your fear and terror will go away.
translation_text.sn11.3:6.1: If you can’t see his banner’s crest, then look up at the banner’s crest of Īsāna, king of gods.
translation_text.sn11.3:6.2: Then your fear and terror will go away.’
translation_text.sn11.3:7.1: However, when they look up at those banner’s crests their fear and terror might go away or it might not.
translation_text.sn11.3:8.2: Because Sakka is not free of greed, hate, and delusion. He is fearful, scared, nervous, quick to flee.
translation_text.sn11.3:9.2: If you’ve gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut and you get scared or terrified, just recollect me:
translation_text.sn11.3:9.3: ‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’
translation_text.sn11.3:9.4: Then your fear and terror will go away.
translation_text.sn11.3:10.1: If you can’t recollect me, then recollect the teaching:
translation_text.sn11.3:10.2: ‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha—apparent in the present life, immediately effective, inviting inspection, relevant, so that sensible people can know it for themselves.’
translation_text.sn11.3:10.3: Then your fear and terror will go away.
translation_text.sn11.3:11.1: If you can’t recollect the teaching, then recollect the Saṅgha:
translation_text.sn11.3:11.2: ‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’
translation_text.sn11.3:11.3: Then your fear and terror will go away.
translation_text.sn11.3:12.2: Because the Realized One is free of greed, hate, and delusion. He is fearless, brave, bold, and stands his ground.”
translation_text.sn11.3:12.3: That is what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn11.3:12.4: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:
translation_text.sn11.3:13.1: “In the wilderness, at a tree’s root,
translation_text.sn11.3:13.2: or an empty hut, O mendicants,
translation_text.sn11.3:13.4: and no fear will come to you.
translation_text.sn11.3:14.1: If you can’t recollect the Buddha—
translation_text.sn11.3:14.2: the eldest in the world, the captain of men—
translation_text.sn11.3:15.1: If you can’t recollect the teaching—
translation_text.sn11.3:16.2: the teaching, and the Saṅgha, mendicants,
translation_text.sn11.3:16.4: and goosebumps will be no more.”
